Here is a table of Persian/Iranian epic and legendary tales involving young heroes facing trials, monsters, or betrayal, mapped for comparative analysis to the figures of Gaumata/Bardiya, Smerdis, Cyrus the Great, Darius I, the six conspirators, and Cambyses. Each entry includes an approximate year (400 BCE) for context, source text with chapter/verse or section where possible, and location.

column3: Faces monsters, demons, betrayal, impossible tasks
Betrays and kills rightful king Jamshid; rules tyrannically, overthrown by Fereydun
Hunted by Zahhak, hidden as a child, returns to overthrow tyrant
Refuses to endorse Zahhak, leads revolt to restore rightful king
Born with white hair, abandoned, raised by Simorgh, returns to claim place

column1: Aži Dahāka (Zahhak) and the Six Conspirators
column3: Conspire to overthrow evil usurper Zahhak
Hidden as a child to escape assassination, raised in obscurity, later revealed
Betrayed and murdered by Cambyses; impostor (Gaumata) takes throne
Plots with six nobles to overthrow the Magian usurper (Gaumata)

column1: The Shahnameh (“Book of Kings”) by Ferdowsi (977–1010 CE) preserves much older oral traditions dating back centuries before Herodotus, often attributed to the pre-Achaemenid and Achaemenid eras, and thus mapped here to c. 400 BCE for comparative mythological context15.
Herodotus’s Histories (written c. 430 BCE) is the main Greek textual source for the stories of Cyrus, Cambyses, Smerdis/Bardiya, and Darius and the six conspirators8.
Location is generally Greater Iran/Persia, with some tales set in regions corresponding to modern Iran and Afghanistan.

These tales reflect the archetype of the hidden or betrayed prince, usurpation, heroic trials, and the restoration of rightful rule-motifs central to both Persian myth and the historical narratives of the Achaemenid dynasty.
If you need more precise references or wish to include other regional variants, let me know!

column1: Cyrus the Great is the only foreign ruler explicitly called “messiah” (anointed) in the Hebrew Bible (Isaiah 45:1), praised for liberating the Jews and enabling the return and rebuilding of the Temple36.
Darius I is depicted as a righteous and supportive king who ensured the completion of the Second Temple and protected the Jewish community, issuing decrees in their favor (Ezra 6:1–15)4.
Darius the Mede (Daniel 6) is portrayed as recognizing the God of Israel’s sovereignty after the deliverance of Daniel, though his historical identity is debated-some equate him with Cyrus46.
Artaxerxes I is also depicted as a benefactor, allowing further restoration and reforms in Jerusalem (Ezra 7; Nehemiah 2).

Other foreign rulers (Nebuchadnezzar, Assyrian kings, Pharaoh Necho, Roman emperors) are generally seen as instruments of God’s plan-sometimes as agents of judgment, sometimes as unwitting fulfillers of prophecy, but not with messianic traits except in the case of Cyrus.

The primary historical source for the Hasmonean and Herodian periods is Flavius Josephus, whose Antiquities of the Jews and The Jewish War provide detailed accounts.
Herodotus is not directly applicable to the Jewish monarchal period, but his Histories provides excellent context on the Achaemenid Persian Empire and its interactions with other cultures.
Hellenistic influence is marked by the adoption of Greek customs, language, architecture, and political institutions.
Persian influence is significant during the Achaemenid period, particularly in the rebuilding of the Second Temple and the granting of autonomy to Jewish communities.
This table tries to be as comprehensive as possible, however depending on the era not all data will be available.
This table aims to provide an easy-to-use resource to compare Jewish monarchs across different eras and cultural influences. Let me know if you need any further refinements!
